islands.name: Classical India
islands.blurb: The Ajanta Caves are 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments dating from the second century BCE to about 480 CE in Maharashtra, India. Universally regarded as masterpieces of Buddhist religious art, their paintings are described as among the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian art, particularly expressive paintings that present emotions through gesture, pose and form.

islands.name: Mughal & Rajput Courts
islands.blurb: Mughal painting is a style of miniature painting, used as book illustrations or as single works kept in albums, which originated during the Mughal period in the Indian subcontinent and reflects an exclusive combination of Indian, Persian and Islamic styles. Alongside it flourished Rajput painting, the painting of the regional Hindu courts during the Mughal era, roughly from the end of the 16th century to the middle of the 19th century.

islands.name: Indian Modern
islands.blurb: The Bengal School of Art was an art movement and a style of Indian painting that originated in Bengal and flourished throughout the Indian subcontinent during the British Raj in the early 20th century. Associated with Indian nationalism (swadeshi) and led by Abanindranath Tagore, it eventually led to the development of modern Indian painting.

artists.name: The Ajanta Painters
artists.bio: The anonymous master painters of the Ajanta Caves decorated 30 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments in the Waghur river gorge of Maharashtra, with the second, more famous phase of work carried out in a brief and brilliant burst of activity around 460–480 CE under the Vakataka emperor Harishena. Universally regarded as masterpieces of Buddhist religious art, their murals present the Jataka tales of the Buddha's former lives through expressive paintings that convey emotion through gesture, pose and form. The caves were gradually abandoned and forgotten until 1819, when a British officer on a hunting party stumbled upon them, and they are today a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Their murals are among the finest surviving examples of ancient Indian art and shaped the whole later tradition of Indian painting.

artists.name: Basawan
artists.bio: Basawan was an Indian miniature painter in the Mughal style, active in the imperial workshop of Emperor Akbar. He was known by his contemporaries as a skilled colorist and keen observer of human nature, and for his use of portraiture in the illustrations of the Akbarnama, Akbar's official biography, which is seen as an innovation in Indian art.
artists.whyTheyMatter: A skilled colorist and keen observer of human nature whose use of portraiture in the Akbarnama is seen as an innovation in Indian art.

artists.name: Ustad Mansur
artists.bio: Ustad Mansur was a seventeenth-century Indian painter and naturalist who served as a Mughal court artist. He excelled at depicting plants and animals, was the earliest artist to depict the dodo in colour, and was the first to illustrate the Siberian crane. Towards the end of Akbar's reign he gained the title of ustad (master), and during the reign of Emperor Jahangir his masterpieces earned him the title of Nadir-al-'Asr ('Wonder of the Age').
artists.whyTheyMatter: The great natural-history painter of the Mughal court — the earliest artist to depict the dodo in colour and the first to illustrate the Siberian crane.

artists.name: Bichitr
artists.bio: Bichitr was an Indian painter during the Mughal period, patronized by the emperors Jahangir and Shah Jahan. The earliest known painting of his is a mature work from c. 1615. Most of his paintings are formal portraits, and a large number of portraits in the 1630s are credited to him. Stuart Cary Welch, noting that he painted the likeness of nearly every important personage from this period, calls him 'the Mughal Van Dyke'.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Court portraitist to Jahangir and Shah Jahan who painted nearly every important personage of his period — 'the Mughal Van Dyke'.

artists.name: Govardhan
artists.bio: Govardhan was a Mughal-era Indian painter of the Mughal school of painting. His father Bhavani Das had been a minor painter in the imperial workshop, and like many other Mughal painters they were Hindus. He joined the imperial service during the reign of Akbar and continued his work through the reigns of Jahangir and Shah Jahan. The examples of his work that are still extant show that he was fond of rich, sensuous colour and softly modelled forms.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Served three emperors from Akbar to Shah Jahan, painting with rich, sensuous colour and softly modelled forms of remarkable human accuracy.

artists.name: Nihal Chand
artists.bio: Nihal Chand was an Indian painter and poet who produced some of the best known examples of Rajput painting. He was the chief painter at the court of Kishangarh during the time of the ruler Savant Singh, and is attributed with a small group of paintings in a distinctive style, mostly depicting the king and Bani Thani as Krishna and Radha. These are widely held to be the finest of all Rajasthani miniatures, and are unusually large for their type.
artists.whyTheyMatter: His Kishangarh paintings of Savant Singh and Bani Thani as Krishna and Radha are widely held to be the finest of all Rajasthani miniatures.

artists.name: Raja Ravi Varma
artists.bio: Raja Ravi Varma was an Indian painter whose works are one of the best examples of the fusion of European academic art with a purely Indian sensibility and iconography. He greatly enhanced his reach and influence as a painter and public figure by making affordable lithographs of his paintings available to the public, which increased the involvement of common people with fine arts. His religious depictions of Hindu deities and works from Indian epic poetry and the Puranas have received critical acclaim, and he was closely related to the royal family of Travancore.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Fused European academic art with a purely Indian sensibility, and his affordable lithographs brought fine art into the homes of ordinary Indians.

artists.name: Abanindranath Tagore
artists.bio: Abanindranath Tagore was an Indian painter who was the principal artist and creator of the Indian Society of Oriental Art in 1907, and the first major exponent of Swadeshi values in Indian art. He founded the influential Bengal school of art, which led to the development of modern Indian painting. Tagore sought to modernise Mughal and Rajput styles to counter the influence of Western models of art as taught under the British Raj, drawing inspiration from Indian art history and the Ajanta Caves; his work was so successful that it was eventually accepted and promoted as a national Indian style within British art institutions.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Founder of the Bengal school of art and the first major exponent of Swadeshi values in Indian art, leading to the development of modern Indian painting.

artists.name: Nandalal Bose
artists.bio: Nandalal Bose was one of the pioneers of modern Indian art and a key figure of Contextual Modernism. A pupil of Abanindranath Tagore, Bose was known for his 'Indian style' of painting and became the principal of Kala Bhavan, Santiniketan in 1921. Influenced by the Tagore family and the murals of Ajanta, his classic works include paintings of scenes from Indian mythologies, women, and village life. He was given the work of illustrating the Constitution of India, and in 1976 the Archaeological Survey of India declared his works among those of the 'nine artists' to be henceforth considered art treasures.
artists.whyTheyMatter: A pioneer of modern Indian art who illustrated the Constitution of India; his works are officially declared national art treasures.

artists.name: Amrita Sher-Gil
artists.bio: Amrita Sher-Gil was a Hungarian–Indian painter who has been called 'one of the greatest avant-garde women artists of the early 20th century' and a pioneer in modern Indian art. Drawn to painting from an early age, she started formal lessons at eight and first gained recognition at nineteen for her 1932 oil painting Young Girls. Sher-Gil depicted the everyday life of the people in her paintings.
artists.whyTheyMatter: Called one of the greatest avant-garde women artists of the early 20th century and a pioneer in modern Indian art.
